judge_result.judge:
@@
A. Freeform text summary of the failure:
After fetching 2018 citations and all paper documents, the agent assigned publisher by naively regex-searching for “(ACM|IEEE|PubMed)” anywhere in each paper’s full text and then averaged citations for the joined “ACM” titles, an incorrect implementation that misclassifies publishers and yields the wrong subset, resulting in 68.5 instead of 46.14473684210526.
B. Failure modes encountered:
FM1: no
FM2: no
FM3: no
FM4: yes
@@

@@
A. Freeform text summary of the failure:
The agent correctly filtered 2018 citations and joined by title, but in execute_python (function-call-13905484818401536990) it naively labeled a paper as ACM if the substring "ACM" appeared anywhere in the text from paper_docs (function-call-2733262756667275774), causing misclassification of publishers and an incorrect average.
B. Failure modes encountered:
FM1: no
FM2: no
FM3: no
FM4: yes
@@

@@
A. Freeform text summary of the failure:
In the execute_python step, the agent used an overly restrictive and incorrect regex (r'Copyright \d{4} \u00a9 (ACM|IEEE|PubMed)') to extract the publisher from paper texts—treating "\u00a9" literally and assuming a specific copyright line—which misidentified ACM sources after merging the 2018 Citations query with paper_docs, leading to an incorrect average of 50.0 instead of 46.14.
B. Failure modes encountered:
FM1: no
FM2: no
FM3: no
FM4: yes
@@

A. Freeform text summary of the failure:
After fetching 2018 citation records and the paper documents, the agent classified ACM-published papers by simply checking if the substring "ACM" appeared anywhere in the paper text (execute_python using results from the two query_db calls), an overly broad parsing rule that misidentified papers and led to an incorrect average.
B. Failure modes encountered:
FM1: no

FM3: no
FM4: yes
@@
